Learn Mode uses spaced retry to help you master every card. Cards you miss are automatically re-queued until you get them right. Test Mode is a timed assessment where you go through the entire deck once, with a detailed report card at the end showing your accuracy, time spent, and which cards you need to review. Both modes include keyboard shortcuts for faster studying. You can also create your own decks and import cards via CSV to quickly build study sets from your lecture notes.

Go beyond memorizing normal ranges. Learn to interpret abnormal lab clusters, analyze arterial blood gases, recognize electrolyte emergencies, and connect diagnostic findings to immediate nursing actions. Each lesson builds the cluster-based reasoning that exam questions demand.

Comprehensive coverage of pregnancy complications, labor and delivery assessment, postpartum care, neonatal danger signs, and pediatric conditions across developmental stages. Understand disease processes at the cellular and molecular level, explained in clinical context. When you know why a condition produces specific signs and symptoms, you can reason through unfamiliar exam questions instead of relying on memorization alone.

Study drug classes organized by mechanism of action, with emphasis on side effect profiles, contraindications, and high-alert medications. Quick recognition drills for transfusion reactions and immediate actions.

Tap the best immediate action. Rationales summarize what to watch for on exams and on the unit.
Practice identifying transfusion reactions and first-line nursing responses.
Rising free hemoglobin or plasma hemoglobin with decreasing haptoglobin (with clinical correlation).
Isolated platelet count drop without other signs.
Stop the transfusion, keep IV access with normal saline, and notify the provider and blood bank per protocol.
Slow the infusion to 50 mL/hr and continue monitoring.
Give antipyretics and continue the transfusion if vital signs stabilize.
A patient develops fever, chills, and flank pain 45 minutes into a red blood cell transfusion. What is your priority?
You suspect acute hemolytic transfusion reaction. Which lab pair best supports hemolysis in this context?
Febrile or hemolytic suspicion requires stopping the blood product, maintaining patent IV access with saline, monitoring, and escalation—before restarting anything.
Hemolysis releases free hemoglobin and consumes haptoglobin; always interpret with the clinical picture and institutional transfusion reaction pathway.
